worth_it_explanation: A buyer that cannot match a bid request back to an authorized seller line in the publisher's ads.txt or app-ads.txt file may discount or skip the impression, so an incorrect or missing Amazon Publisher Services (APS) line can suppress demand for that inventory. The check is a read of files the publisher already controls, compared against the seller account details APS issues, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time. The finding points to a specific line to add, correct, or remove.
intro: Authorized Digital Sellers, the standard behind the ads.txt and app-ads.txt files, lets a publisher publish a list of the accounts authorized to sell its inventory so buyers can confirm a bid request comes from a legitimate seller rather than a spoofed one. APS sells the publisher's inventory through its server-side marketplaces, so APS is a declared seller and its account details belong in those files. This subcategory checks that the APS lines in ads.txt and app-ads.txt match the seller account APS issued and that no stale or unauthorized APS lines remain. The IAB Tech Lab maintains the ads.txt and app-ads.txt specifications these checks reference.

audit_checks.title: The Amazon Publisher Services seller line is missing or carries the wrong account ID or relationship
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the ads.txt or app-ads.txt file lacks a correct Amazon Publisher Services seller line, buyers cannot verify that APS is authorized to sell the inventory, so they may discount or skip the bid request. A wrong account ID or a DIRECT or RESELLER designation that does not match the APS arrangement breaks the same verification, which can suppress demand for inventory that is otherwise sound.

educate.base:
The Authorized Digital Sellers standard from the IAB Tech Lab is a plain text file, named ads.txt for web inventory and app-ads.txt for app inventory, that a publisher posts at the root of its domain to list every account authorized to sell its inventory. Each line names the advertising system's domain, the seller account ID inside that system, a relationship of DIRECT or RESELLER, and an optional certification authority ID. A buyer reads the file before bidding and confirms the bid request comes from an account the publisher listed, which is how the standard separates legitimate supply from spoofed inventory.

Amazon Publisher Services (APS) sells the publisher's inventory through its server-side marketplaces, so APS is a declared seller and its line belongs in the file. The relationship designation records how the publisher works with that seller. DIRECT means the publisher holds the account directly with the advertising system named on the line, and RESELLER means an authorized third party resells the inventory on the publisher's behalf. The line is correct only when the advertising system domain, the account ID APS issued, and the relationship all match the arrangement the publisher actually has.

When the APS line is missing, a buyer reading the file finds no authorization for APS to sell the inventory, so the bid request fails the check the standard was built to run. A wrong account ID points the buyer at an account that is not the publisher's, and a relationship that does not match, for example DIRECT recorded where the arrangement is a reseller, conflicts with the seller record APS publishes in its sellers.json file. Each of these breaks verification on inventory that is otherwise legitimate.

A few signs point to a missing or incorrect APS line:
  • The ads.txt or app-ads.txt file has no line for the APS advertising system domain at all
  • An APS line exists but its account ID does not match the seller account ID APS issued the publisher
  • The relationship on the APS line reads DIRECT or RESELLER in a way that does not match how the publisher works with APS or what APS declares in sellers.json
You may want to confirm the APS line against the seller account details in the APS portal, checking the advertising system domain, the account ID, and the relationship each in turn. One approach is to copy the exact line APS supplies in the portal rather than hand-typing it, so a transposed digit or a wrong relationship does not slip in.

site_type_overlays.publishing: A media publisher that sells inventory across several properties may run one ads.txt file per domain, so the APS line needs to be present and correct on each domain that carries APS demand rather than on the flagship alone. You may want to check every property's file because a missing line on a secondary domain suppresses APS demand only there and can go unseen while the main site looks fine.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A commerce-content publisher monetizing editorial pages alongside its store may serve ads only on part of the domain, but ads.txt is read at the domain root regardless of which sections carry ads. You may want to confirm the APS line sits in the root ads.txt file even when the monetized pages live under a subpath because buyers look only at the root.

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed instructions for the exact ads.txt and app-ads.txt line to publish, including the seller account ID issued to the publisher, are in the APS partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Open the publisher's ads.txt and app-ads.txt files, find the lines for the Amazon Publisher Services advertising system, and compare each field against the seller account details APS supplies in the portal.

ads_txt_file.label: ads.txt and app-ads.txt files
steps: Open the ads.txt file at the root of each domain that carries APS demand, e.g., 'example.com/ads.txt', and the app-ads.txt file for any app inventory.
Find every line whose first field is the Amazon Publisher Services advertising system domain, and record the account ID and the relationship on each.
Compare the account ID on the APS line against the seller account ID APS issued the publisher in the portal, and note any mismatch.
Confirm the relationship of DIRECT or RESELLER on the line matches how the publisher works with APS, and note any line where the relationship is missing or does not match.

sellers_json_lookup.label: Amazon Publisher Services sellers.json
steps: Look up the publisher's seller account in the APS sellers.json file to see how APS declares the relationship for that account.
Compare the relationship APS declares against the DIRECT or RESELLER designation on the publisher's ads.txt line, and note any case where the two disagree.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each domain and app inventory, whether an Amazon Publisher Services line is present in ads.txt or app-ads.txt and whether its advertising system domain, account ID, and relationship match the seller account APS issued and the relationship APS declares in sellers.json. A file that carries a correct, matching APS line on every property that runs APS demand passes. A missing line, a wrong account ID, or a relationship that conflicts with the APS arrangement or sellers.json is a fail recorded with the file and field affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a correct Amazon Publisher Services line is present in each ads.txt and app-ads.txt file, with its account ID and relationship, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} is missing a correct Amazon Publisher Services (APS) seller line in its ads.txt or app-ads.txt file, or that the line carries an account ID or relationship that does not match the APS arrangement. Buyers read these files to confirm a seller is authorized before bidding, so a missing or wrong APS line breaks that check and can suppress demand for inventory that is otherwise sound. {{voice_recommend}} copying the exact APS line from the partner portal into the root ads.txt file for each property that runs APS demand, and into app-ads.txt for app inventory, then confirming the account ID and the DIRECT or RESELLER relationship match what APS declares in its sellers.json record. After the change, recheck each file to confirm the line is present and correct.

audit_checks.title: Stale, duplicate, or unauthorized Amazon Publisher Services lines remain in the file
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Duplicate Amazon Publisher Services lines add no authorization a single line does not already grant, but a stale line for an account the publisher no longer uses, or an unauthorized line added without the publisher's knowledge, leaves the file declaring a seller that should not be there. That weakens the transparency the file exists to provide and can route demand through a path the publisher did not intend.

educate.base:
An ads.txt file grows over time as a publisher adds sellers, tests partners, and migrates between accounts, and lines that were added once tend to stay unless someone removes them. The Authorized Digital Sellers standard treats every line in the file as a live authorization, so a line for an Amazon Publisher Services (APS) account the publisher stopped using still tells buyers that account is authorized to sell the inventory. A clean file lists only the sellers the publisher uses today.

Three patterns leave the file declaring more than it should. A duplicate line repeats an APS authorization the file already grants, which adds clutter and makes the file harder to read without changing what it authorizes. A stale line authorizes an APS account the publisher has retired, for example after closing an old seller account or moving to a new one, so the file keeps vouching for a path the publisher no longer sells through. An unauthorized line authorizes an APS account the publisher never approved, which can appear when a line is copied from another publisher's file or added by a partner without the publisher's sign-off.

The risk rises from duplicate to stale to unauthorized. Duplicates are mostly housekeeping. A stale line keeps a closed account live in the eyes of buyers, which is a transparency gap the publisher can close by deleting the line. An unauthorized line is the more serious case because it declares a seller the publisher did not choose and can route demand through a path the publisher did not intend, which is exactly the spoofing the standard was built to prevent.

A few signs point to lines that should be removed:
  • The same APS advertising system domain and account ID appear on more than one line
  • A line authorizes an APS account that maps to a partner or account the publisher no longer works with
  • A line names an APS account ID the publisher does not recognize and cannot trace to its own seller records in the portal
You may want to reconcile every APS line in the file against the publisher's current APS seller accounts, keeping the lines that match a live account and removing duplicates, retired accounts, and any line that cannot be traced to an account the publisher approved. One approach is to treat the APS portal's current account list as the source of truth and delete any APS line in the file that the portal does not account for.

site_type_overlays.publishing: A media publisher whose ads.txt file has accumulated entries across years of partner tests may find several old APS lines from accounts it no longer uses. You may want to date the cleanup against the publisher's APS account history, removing lines tied to accounts closed long ago rather than assuming every present line is current.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A commerce-content publisher that manages its own ads.txt file alongside store operations may have had APS lines added by an outside partner during setup. You may want to confirm each APS line traces to an account the publisher holds in the portal because a line added by a partner without sign-off is the kind of unauthorized entry the standard is meant to flag.

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's record of the seller accounts issued to the publisher, used to confirm which APS lines are current, is in the APS partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: List every Amazon Publisher Services line in the ads.txt and app-ads.txt files, then reconcile each against the publisher's current APS seller accounts to find duplicates, retired accounts, and lines that cannot be traced.

ads_txt_file.label: ads.txt and app-ads.txt files
steps: Open the ads.txt file at each domain root and the app-ads.txt file for app inventory, and list every line whose advertising system domain is Amazon Publisher Services.
Flag any APS advertising system domain and account ID that appears on more than one line as a duplicate.
For each remaining APS line, record the account ID so it can be checked against the publisher's current APS accounts.

aps_account_reconcile.label: Amazon Publisher Services account reconciliation
steps: Pull the list of current APS seller accounts the publisher holds from the portal.
Match each APS line in the file to a current account, and flag any line whose account ID maps to a retired account or cannot be traced to any account the publisher holds.
Record the duplicate, stale, and untraceable lines together as the set to remove.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner lists every Amazon Publisher Services line in the files and reconciles each against the publisher's current APS seller accounts. A file where every APS line traces to a live account and no line is duplicated passes. Any duplicate line, any line tied to a retired account, or any line whose account ID cannot be traced to an account the publisher holds is a fail recorded with the line and the reason it should be removed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each Amazon Publisher Services line in the files and whether it traces to a current APS account above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} keeps Amazon Publisher Services (APS) lines in its ads.txt or app-ads.txt file that should not be there, whether duplicates, lines for retired accounts, or lines that cannot be traced to an account the publisher holds. Every line in the file reads as a live authorization to buyers, so a stale or unauthorized APS line keeps vouching for a seller the publisher no longer uses or never approved, which weakens the transparency the file exists to provide. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling each APS line against the current seller accounts in the portal, then removing duplicates, lines tied to closed accounts, and any line that cannot be traced to an account the publisher holds. After the cleanup, confirm the file lists only the APS accounts the publisher uses today.
